topic: Trump Deal Saudis Nuclear Delusional Critics

left.title: 'Delusional': Critics explode over Trump deal with Saudis that could set off nuclear race

left-center.title: Trump Administration to Announce Deal That Could Let Saudis Enrich Nuclear Fuel

farLeftText: President Donald Trump spurred terror and outrage Wednesday when reports surfaced that he formally approved a sweeping 30-year nuclear agreement with Saudi Arabia, worth tens of billions of dollars, that could pave the way for uranium enrichment in the Middle East. The accord, expected to be signed Wednesday by Energy Secretary Chris Wright and Saudi Prince Abdulaziz bin Salman, gives American companies a central role in building Saudi Arabia's civilian nuclear infrastructure while locking out foreign competitors like Russia and China, but nonproliferation experts warn the deal could trigger a cascade of nuclear ambitions across the Middle East, reported the Wall Street Journal. "Whither goes Saudi Arabia, so goes the United Arab Emirates, Turkey and Egypt," said Henry Sokolski, executive director of the Nonproliferation Policy Education Center. "The idea that this will have a happy ending is delusional." A key provision would allow U.S. companies to construct a uranium-enrichment facility in Saudi Arabia if a joint two-year study determines the step is warranted, though the facility would operate under a "black box" arrangement designed to keep sensitive enrichment technology out of Saudi hands. Sokolski and other critics say that framework doesn't go far enough, warning that Saudi Arabia's refusal to accept the so-called "gold standard" — a binding commitment never to enrich uranium or reprocess spent fuel domestically — sets a dangerous precedent. Robert Einhorn, a former senior State Department nonproliferation official, echoed those concerns, saying that without adequate constraints on enrichment, the deal "could increase nuclear proliferation risks in the Middle East and beyond." Adding to the alarm is Saudi Arabia's rejection of the International Atomic Energy Agency's "Additional Protocol," which would have subjected the kingdom to more rigorous inspections. Critics argue the alternative monitoring arrangement negotiated by the Trump administration will be insufficient to catch suspected nuclear activity at sites not directly built by the U.S. Congressional critics have also raised objections. Rep. Brad Sherman (D-CA) said lawmakers should withhold approval unless Saudi Arabia agrees to forgo enrichment and reprocessing entirely, arguing the administration shouldn't enable proliferation risks "just because U.S. companies stand to make a profit." The deal also arrives as the White House presses Iran, Saudi Arabia's regional rival, to sharply limit its own nuclear program — a juxtaposition critics call contradictory, even as Trump officials insist the Saudi program will be safeguarded. Blocking the agreement will be difficult because doing so requires a joint congressional resolution capable of overriding a presidential veto, a two-thirds majority threshold rarely cleared on foreign policy disputes. The deal is expected to reach Congress for review in the coming days. Presidents from both parties have long objected to efforts by Middle Eastern countries to enrich uranium, and some lawmakers have argued that a nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia, in particular, was such a sensitive topic that it should face an even tougher congressional standard. A bipartisan group of lawmakers – including now-Secretary of State Marco Rubio – in 2018 sponsored an act that would block nuclear cooperation with the kingdom unless Congress affirmatively voted to approve such a deal, but that measure was never brought to a vote.

topic: Democrats 2028 Presidential Calendar South Carolina

left-center.title: South Carolina and Nevada Fight to Kick Off Democrats’ 2028 Presidential Calendar

center.title: Democrats could take initial vote on 2028 presidential primary calendar this week

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.

centerText:
The Rules and Bylaws Committee has been holding monthly meetings as it considers the order in which states will vote during the 2028 primaries. The panel has heard presentations from a dozen states hoping to kick off the party's primaries and caucuses, including Iowa, Michigan, New Hampshire, Nevada and South Carolina.

The panel that sets the party's voting order could make that determination as early as Friday when it meets in Washington, two people with knowledge of the party's process but not authorized to discuss it publicly told The Associated Press on Monday.

COLUMBIA, S.C. (AP) — Democrats could vote as early as this week on the order of their primary calendar for the 2028 presidential election, although the decision wouldn't be final until the full Democratic National Committee agrees.

The calendar isn't considered set until it's approved by the full DNC, which is scheduled to meet in August. The DNC did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

Democrats upended their process for setting their calendar after Iowa's 2020 Democratic caucus debacle, which led to days of uncertainty over who had won. The party since then has favored more diverse states.

For the 2024 cycle, President Joe Biden led a DNC effort to have South Carolina — which for years has held the party's first Southern contest — go first overall in the party's primaries. He cited the state's more racially diverse population compared to the traditional first-in-the-nation states of Iowa and New Hampshire, which are overwhelmingly white.

Four years earlier, Rep. Jim Clyburn, South Carolina's lone congressional Democrat and for a time the top Black Democratic lawmaker on Capitol Hill, helped Biden revive his flagging primary campaign and win a resounding victory in the state. He then went on to secure the party's presidential nomination.

South Carolina's Democratic leaders have pushed for the state to lead off again, lobbying for support and getting buy-in from Democratic Party chairs in five Southern states. In May, they pressed for South Carolina to reprise its role as the party's first-in-the-nation contest holder, arguing that the state best represents the initial playing field for presidential candidates to build the coalitions needed to win.

A quarter of the state's population is Black and about 9% is Hispanic, according to the Census Bureau.

topic: Russian Online Retailer Wildberries Ukraine Targets

left-center.title: Ukraine targets Russian online retailer Wildberries

center.title: Ukrainian drones hit Russian online giant retailer Wildberries for second time

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText: Ukrainian long-range drones targeted large warehouses belonging to Wildberries, Russia’s equivalent of Amazon, which sells military equipment like body armor and drone parts, but mainly serves as a place for regular Russians to sell their goods. President Zelenskyy said the warehouses are “involved in supplying the Russian army,” adding that Ukraine is “brining the war home – to Russia.”July 22, 2026
centerText:
Workers were evacuated after the attacks on logistics centres in Krasnodar and Nevinnomyssk, Wildberries founder and Russia's wealthiest woman Tatyana Kim said earlier on Wednesday.

"There are no words to describe all the feelings and emotions caused by the attacks on ordinary people, on our employees, on us, who are simply doing our jobs," she wrote on Telegram. "But these situations demonstrate the most important thing: we must continue to do what we do for the benefit of people."

In Krasnodar, 10 people were injured in an attack on a warehouse complex, with three in a serious condition, the region's Governor Veniamin Kondratyev said.

He later said that a young woman died of her injuries in hospital.

Five people were hurt in a warehouse complex on the outskirts of Nevinnomyssk, Stavropol Governor Vladimir Vladimirov said.

BBC Verify has analysed footage and confirmed fires in the two locations.

Several hours after the latest attacks, a range of military gear - including body armour and drone components - was widely available on Wildberries' website, a quick search by the BBC showed.

Serhii Kuzan, chairman of the Ukrainian Security and Cooperation Centre, has described Wildberries as a "vital component" of Russian logistics, adding that the firm's website is being used by Russian volunteers to buy military equipment.

"The primary rationale for striking Wildberries' warehouses is to disrupt Russian logistics and the supply of dual-use goods, critical electronics, sanctioned goods and the like to the Russian army and Russian arms manufacturers," Kuzan told the BBC.

"The collateral damage from such strikes could also have a serious impact on the Russian economy, as well as a psychological effect on Russian society and, likely, on continued support for the war."

The Ukrainian strikes have also dealt a serious blow to Russia's small and mid-sized businesses, angering hundreds of sellers whose livelihoods depend on online marketplaces.

topic: Mykhailo Drapatyi Ukraines Know General Commanderinchief

left-center.title: Mykhailo Drapatyi: What to Know About Ukraine’s New Top General

center.title: Who is Mykhailo Drapatyi, Ukraine's new commander-in-chief?

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerText: Maj Gen Mykhailo Drapatyi, 43, has replaced Oleksandr Syrskyi as Ukraine's commander-in-chief - the third since the start of Russia's full-scale invasion in 2022. His nomination has been welcomed by both civil society and the military. Drapatyi's name was chanted during protests that started off backing his friend, ex-defence minister Mykhailo Fedorov, but ended up demanding Syrskyi's dismissal. "There's no better person for this position nowadays in Ukraine," one Ukrainian soldier told the BBC. Drapatyi's support rests on years of military experience in post-Soviet Ukraine, his skills as field general and his reputation for being a humane and strong commander among the military. He graduated from the Kharkiv Institute of Tank Troops in 2004 and began serving as a lieutenant. Ten years later he gained prominence when footage went viral showing his infantry fighting vehicle smashing through a makeshift barricade. It was May 2014 and pro-Russian separatists had taken over parts of eastern Ukraine, when Drapatiy's battalion entered the southern city of Mariupol to free hostages held in the city's police department. He later recalled being told there were barricades more than a metre high ahead and being asked what to do. "I said, let's take it by storm. This video flew around the world," he said in an interview. Later that year, he was commended for withdrawing encircled Ukrainian units from a border area of the Luhansk region. Drapatiy returned to university in 2021 for further operational and strategic training and was presented with a sword of honour as top graduate in June 2021 by British ambassador Melinda Simmons. When Russia's full-scale invasion began in February 2022, Drapatiy was deputy commander of joint forces. He led operational groups - temporary task forces - in several areas of the front line, successfully pushing back the advance of Russian troops towards Kryvyi Rih and liberating a number of settlements in the Kherson and Dnipropretrovsk regions. By 2024 he was heading the land troops of the Ukrainian army and leading task forces in some of the east's most intense combat areas. Less than a year later, a training ground in the Dnipropetrovsk region was targeted by a Russian missile and 12 soldiers were killed and 60 others were wounded in the strike. Drapatyi handed in his resignation the very same day, saying he took personal responsibility for the "conspiracy of silence and impunity" that had led to the deaths. " "An army in which commanders bear personal responsibility for the lives of personnel keeps living. An army in which nobody is responsible for a loss dies," he wrote. It was seen as a sign of personal accountability, and Zelensky's decision to instead appoint him commander of the Joint Forces of the Armed Forces of Ukraine was widely praised. From then on, whispered rumours of tensions with Syrskyi grew louder. "Drapatyi is a relatively young general. He is gifted. He was always sent to places where the situation was extremely difficult," one unnamed officer told Ukrainian media in June 2025. "I do not think he has political ambitions. Yet I am sure his ambitions are not confined to the position of commander of the ground forces or the Joint Forces." Just over a year later, Syrskyi has gone, and hopes for Drapatyi are high. "Drapatyi has a very good reputation among the rank and file - especially those who joined after 2022 as civilians," says Mykola Bielieskov, senior analyst at the Ukrainian NGO Come Back Alive. But he notes Drapatyi will have to operate under serious constraints. "The Commander-in-Chief has a lot of leverage. But war is a combination of government, the president's office, military suppliers, dependence on [Western] partners... so he's got a tricky path before him." Prospects of a ceasefire with Russia remain slim and Ukraine faces another harsh winter of war. Mobilising manpower is a priority, and Drapatyi will also be expected to tackle the corruption that touches all levels of the army, from conscription officers to defence procurement. Meanwhile, he will have to capitalise on Ukraine's remarkable technological advances and the burgeoning tech defence industry that has spearheaded Ukraine's recent battlefield successes. In his first message after being appointed Commander-in-Chief, Drapatyi acknowledged the difficulty of his task and promised to work "without making loud promises, with responsibility for our decisions, and with respect" for those on the front line. Many will be looking for a change from what was seen as Syrskyi's "outdated" way of fighting, which placed little value on human life in favour of brutal offensives. "It's a major role and people will expect change quickly," analyst Bielieskov said, warning against "oversimplification" that can lead to demand for sweeping improvements in an unrealistic length of time. This has been echoed by former interior ministry adviser Oleksiy Kopytko. As commander-in-chief, he noted, "you are worshipped and loved by all today, not loved by everyone tomorrow, and are guilty of everything the day after tomorrow, with none of your achievements mattering". Valery Zaluzhnyi, who was removed as army chief in 2024, predicted that Drapatyi would "have a very difficult time. Much harder than he imagines". But among many soldiers and ordinary Ukrainians there is a tangible sense of excitement for the new shift in outlook that Drapatyi represents. One said that if Mykhailo Fedorov were reinstated as defence minister, his and Drapatyi's leadership would be "Russia's worst nightmare". When he resigned from his position in 2025, Drapatyi listed issues he had tackled in the Ground Forces - ranging from combat training reform to unfair mobilisation, corruption and flawed procurement systems. "There is expected resistance, but efforts need to be stepped up," he said at the time. He will face similar problems in his new role - albeit on a much larger scale and under the eyes of the whole country. Additional material by BBC Monitoring

topic: Trump Strike Pickaxe Mountain Threatened Suspected

left-center.title: Trump Threatened to Strike Pickaxe Mountain, a Suspected Iranian Nuclear Site. Here’s What to Know.

right.title: Trump: U.S. ‘Not Finished at All’ in Iran; Pickaxe Mountain Strike Coming ‘Pretty Soon’

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerRightText: No article found in this category for this topic.
farRightText:
President Donald Trump declared Tuesday that the United States is “not finished at all” with Iran, warning U.S. forces would strike the regime’s heavily fortified Pickaxe Mountain nuclear site “pretty soon” while signaling his administration was prepared to expand military operations rather than return to negotiations.

Trump made the remarks while meeting Lebanese President Joseph Aoun at the White House after being asked about a Wall Street Journal report that Israeli intelligence believes Iran moved thousands of advanced centrifuges into the underground Pickaxe Mountain facility near Natanz last fall.

“I think that they may have,” Trump said when asked about the reported transfer, while stressing that the centrifuges alone would not allow Iran to revive its nuclear program.

“It doesn’t mean anything unless they have the material. They don’t have it. We follow the material. That’s where the action is.”

“We’ll be hitting that area probably pretty soon. There’s not a thing they can do about it,” he said. “Normally, I wouldn’t say that. If I thought they could do something about it, I would never say that. But we’ll be hitting that area pretty soon, and very heavily.”

Pickaxe Mountain, located near Iran’s Natanz nuclear complex, has become an increasing focus of U.S. and Israeli concern because of its extensive underground fortifications. Israeli intelligence reportedly believes Iran transferred advanced centrifuges to the site, while international inspectors have long sought greater access amid concerns the mountain could house undeclared nuclear activities.

Trump suggested any attempt by Tehran to rebuild its nuclear infrastructure would simply invite additional American strikes.

“How about these people? They’re in this because of nuclear weapons, and they’re trying to possibly reconstitute a site? We’ll hit that site,” Trump said. “Any site where they’re even thinking about nuclear, we’ll be hitting it very, very powerfully.”

Trump also made clear the broader U.S. campaign against Iran was far from over, arguing the damage inflicted by American strikes would take Tehran decades to overcome.

“If we left right now, it would take Iran 20 to 25 years to rebuild,” Trump said. “We’re not finished at all… we’re not leaving right now.”

Trump likewise brushed aside suggestions that Washington was preparing to pivot back toward diplomacy, saying Tehran was seeking talks only because of sustained military pressure.

“They want to desperately meet because they’re getting decimated,” Trump said. “Until they’re ready to meet in a meaningful way, we have no interest.”

Trump’s remarks came as U.S. forces continued striking Iranian military infrastructure, with U.S. Central Command announcing additional operations targeting missile and drone capabilities, military command centers, maritime assets, air defense systems, and other facilities tied to Tehran’s efforts to disrupt commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. Trump has repeatedly argued the campaign is intended to prevent Iran from rebuilding its nuclear program while maintaining military pressure on the regime.

Asked about Iran’s remaining capabilities, Trump maintained the administration would continue applying military pressure while monitoring Tehran’s nuclear assets.

“We’re degrading them at levels that nobody thought was possible,” he said, adding that U.S. officials continue to track Iran’s nuclear material rather than focusing solely on centrifuges or infrastructure.

Trump closed with another warning that the administration was prepared to intensify operations if Iran attempted to revive its nuclear program.

“If we left today, it would take them decades to rebuild. I don’t believe they could ever rebuild,” Trump said. “Honestly, they haven’t seen anything yet. We’ve been nice.”

topic: Venezuelas Maduro Court Pretrial Hearing Case

left.title: Venezuela’s Maduro in court for pre-trial hearing in US drug-trafficking case

center.title: Venezuela's Nicolás Maduro back to court for pretrial hearing in his U.S. drug trafficking case

farLeftText: Nicolás Maduro returns to court with his lawyers on Wednesday and is expected to continue objecting to the US military action that snatched the Venezuelan president from power and deposited him in New York to face drug-trafficking charges. Maduro, 63, and his wife Cilia Flores, 69, have been held at a Brooklyn jail ever since US forces seized them from their Caracas home in a stunning middle-of-the-night raid and brought them to New York in early January. Both have pleaded not guilty. They face potential life prison sentences if a jury agrees that they were part of a conspiracy to send cocaine into the US. Donald Trump’s administration has defended the raid as a “surgical law enforcement operation” in a criminal case first brought six years ago. Maduro has called himself a prisoner of war and his capture a kidnapping. US prosecutors say Maduro carried out a conspiracy to move thousands of tons of cocaine into the US by colluding with Venezuelan law enforcement to help drug kingpins. “I am not guilty. I am a decent man, the constitutional president of my country,” he said in Spanish at his arraignment in January. Maduro’s lawyer, Barry Pollack, has said he expects to contest the legality of Maduro’s “military abduction”. The lawyers have said there are complex legal defenses that the court must address before a trial can begin. Maduro and Flores have not asked to be released on bail. Judge Alvin K Hellerstein has yet to set a trial date.

NEW YORK (AP) — Nicolás Maduro returns to court with his lawyers Wednesday and is expected to continue objecting to the U.S. military action that snatched the Venezuelan president from power and deposited him in New York to face drug trafficking charges.

Maduro, 63, and his wife Cilia Flores, 69, have been held at a Brooklyn jail ever since U.S. forces seized them from their Caracas home in a stunning middle-of-the-night raid and brought them to New York in early January.

centerText:
Bodycam and dashcam footage shows a police officer rescue a man seconds before his car was engulfed in flames near Dallas, Texas.

Corporal Joshua Swisher was conducting a traffic stop on a motorway when he was alerted to a nearby vehicle emitting smoke.

Shortly after directing the vehicle to the emergency lane, flames erupted from underneath it while the driver was still inside.

Newly-released footage shows the incident, which happened on 7 June.
